displayTitle: 2026-06-18 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - vimeo_1202266008
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee

chunks.text: Minister, we have been waiting since 8:30 for you. Oh, I apologize. I had 8:45 down. My apologies. Oh, okay. All right. So there is a mix-up. So Minister, thank you so much for your time. We- Oh, first I must apologize. Sincere apologies. I thought it was 8:45.

All right, so we had 45 minutes for you, but now it will be only around 30 minutes. If you would like to start with some opening remarks, and if you could keep them really, really brief, because we don't have enough time left for questions. Thank you. Yeah. Oh well, I'll just take everything as read. This is a different fund than the period of time over the last government and PGF.

This particular fund is operated under different fiscal strictures. A third of it, roughly speaking, has been grant financed. The majority of it has been bound by demanding guardrails related to either debt financing instruments or co-investment.

I don't mind saying, that has led to more protracted processes, but that is the outcome when you are waiting for your co-investment partners to mobilize the capital before the crown capital is invested. At a highlight higher level, about $200 million has gone into stop bank work.

That's co-investment with regional councils. About 120 million into energy. About 60 million carrying on from former Minister Megan Woods, super hot critical geothermal energy. About 50 million was put aside to de-risk and address the acceleration of geothermal infrastructure related matters, whether it's covering of the early stages of geothermal projects.
About 150 odd million for additional water storage, including the formerly known Ruataniwha, much vexed Tukituki. 80 million for critical minerals.

Belatedly, an attempt to do something for aviation connectivity, about 30 million. I've got to say, I'd welcome any questions about that because I think it's a really tricky area for the Crown to be involved in. Projects that you might put a moko on, call them Maori development projects, about 40 million.

They come with their own complications, which I'm sure you're all aware of. $200 million, the gas security fund, which has struck a few problems, which I'll gladly elaborate upon, relating to a court case late last year. I only raise that because it's under the administration of the Karnoor group.

And yeah, a variety of other projects. And look, there are other projects that are subject to Karnoor's administration. But if you wanted an indication of some of the big amounts of dough are, Madam Chair, perhaps I would leave that as an introductory set of remarks.

Thank you, Minister. So members, our minister is here in his capacity as the Minister for Regional Development until 9:15, and after that, as Minister for Resources. So I just want to make sure that the questions are split accordingly. So we have questions. We start with the Honorable Damien O'Connor. Look, thank you, Minister, and while appreciating the value of these funds over time, keeping tabs on them, it hasn't been easy. And some people say it's Jonesy's slush fund, and I think you're proud of much of the growth. But I guess from a taxpayer's perspective,

keeping tabs on it is important. One of the performance measures have been removed, percentage of contracted projects monitored at key contract stages. It's been taken out, and the reason is, and I'm wondering whether you've written this yourself because it's so eloquent. If I can just say, the vote notes the rationale for removing this measure is that, quote, "The performance indicator and budget standard was removed for '26, '27, and out years as a measure is already in place to establish a basic auditable portfolio level contract monitoring

baseline by tracking evidence, monitoring, and movement through contract stages." Could you explain what that means? Yeah. I'll do one even better. We've got about 145 million remaining in the fund. So I'll go down to figures, so you know exactly how much dough is left, and I'll come back to putting in plain English what we think we're doing.

We've committed about a billion dollars. We've contracted about 450 million. Sadly, we've only paid out about 100 million. So I'm not happy about that. But I go back to the fact the moment that you're spending most of the money via co-investment or debt instruments as opposed to subsidies or grants, the simplest thing for the Crown to do in this area of expenditure is subsidy or grant. The money just goes out the door.

But the government that I belong to decided that they would move away from that historic approach And go with a co-investment model, the belief that it represented more discipline. That slows the process down. And then the trade-off is, in slowing it down, are you reducing your impact on the economy whilst trying to save dough?

i.e., save yourself from the prospect of throwing dough at the wrong thing. In relation to how much dough has been sought, about 1.9 billion was applied for from a $1.2 billion fund. The key point that we're endeavoring to do is, and I want to be straight up with you, Mr.

O'Connor, had this fund had a different split and more was grant or subsidy funding, more dough would've gone out the door. And that perhaps represents the key difference between the PGF and the current fund. So, when we use the language you've just described, it's an attempt to say that monitor it, but the monitoring really doesn't mean much in terms of the impact on the ground until the money has been raised by my co-investment partners.

Let's use an example. Cabinet signed off on 30-odd million dollars on the nuclear fusion project. That money will never see the light of day in the event that, the lad, Ratu, I think his name is- Vincent... Mataira, until he raises the international capital from America or wherever, then the crown capital will flow.

So, at one level, we've made the announcement, but the impact on jobs, the impact on the economy, will only be measurable when he raises his money. Supplementary? Yeah. Yeah, supplementary. I'd be interested to pursue that one as a question, but I don't want to follow individual projects.

If I can come back to regional development per se, if you were to take out the record commodity prices for dairy and the record commodity prices for beef, how well is regional development going? Well, the focus on this fund is infrastructure. I think that the South Island regional economies are outstripping other regional economies. I think the East Coast is a basket case.

I think it's a basket case because of problems that our local government institution has up there trying to implement forestry regulations, amongst other things. I won't to talk about my own area, but I do think that we have certain pockets around New Zealand where we've got elements of dysfunctionalism which are blighting the growth.

I think Taranaki's got a big problem with the closure and the rapid decline of oil and gas. But in the South Island, I think we've almost got, economically speaking, two different countries. Could I-- Thank you. And if you look at the closures of manufacturing plants, particularly around forestry, lack of profitability in the wine industry, it is, as you say, perhaps two economies and, as I say, take away the opportunistic pricing for dairy and beef. The question comes back to, do you think, in spite of your efforts,

that the rest of the government's policy settings are assisting regional development, or indeed, are they hampering it, given you have made reference to electricity and the structure of that market? Yeah. Well, fellow MPs, the reason why we've thrown quite a bit of dough at energy, and I genuinely believe it is infrastructure, is that I have been told when I go around the country in regions, a key impediment to growing economic enterprises is the cost of electricity.

Mm. I'm told this by a variety of voices across the spectrum of the economy and the spectrum of political forces. So, I went back to our colleagues and said, "We've got to do something to accelerate the delivery of energy." I have zero confidence in the gentailers. You must be aware of that. I was unsuccessful in securing the energy portfolio because the party I belong to only got 6% vote.

I'm of the view that our economy is on a downward spiral unless we can deliver a set of outcomes that boosts the affordability of electricity in New Zealand. So- Yep... thank you very much. I appreciate the percentage vote that you have, but you've been able to leverage quite a lot of things through government, through your position in Cabinet.

Why have you not been able to make any progress around electricity? Well, I think, if you look at the pretty big amount of dough that we've thrown at electricity-orientated projects, they do take a long time, and we are waiting for my colleague, Simeon, to bring back a recommendation as to what do we do to strengthen the firming market in New Zealand.

And we're waiting for Simeon, my colleague, to bring us back a recommendation. And I know that it's not really my wheelhouse, and I know he's been-- I don't know if it was this committee or some committee, and you guys had a good go at it, so I don't want to follow the Chair's admonition not to stray too far. So I'll leave that corridor over there.

But I would plead with you, Madam Chair, we've spent a lot of money out of this fund on energy, but I'd ask that you accept that it takes several years before these projects come to fruition. But I say it again Our economy is going to hollow out due to climate alarmism and the escalating cost of energy.

All right. One- Thank you. One more. I guess, in terms of regional development, one of the things that's been put forward, I think in Southland and other places, is the development of data centers. So given the current existing and uninterrupted electricity market, do you think that the development of that data center, and more of them, will assist other users of electricity across the country?

Well, I've been told that data centers in Auckland have left the field of play because they couldn't get through the RMA. The one down in Southland, I've been briefed by it. I've met the promoter on numerous occasions, and providing that they can do a deal on energy that doesn't escalate the cost of energy to every other New Zealander, that's not a bad idea. But once again, I say that no one in New Zealand is responsible for energy security.

We're dreaming if we believe we can outsource to the gentailers the sovereign role of energy security in New Zealand. We're dreaming if we believe we can outsource energy resilience to the gentailers. They do not exist for that purpose. They exist for the purpose of net profit after tax. It's only the Crown that exists for energy national security, and sadly, successive governments have outsourced that sovereign role to the private market, and I mean to change that in the event that we're privileged to govern again.

All right. New question or primary, Scott Willis? Supplementary. Supplementary to-- Yeah, Scott Willis. So in relation to the data center, and given that we do not have that secure firming, and we do not have the answer from government, do you still support the Southland data center proceeding, knowing that that will cause greater hardship, knowing that that is going to have an impact on electricity prices?

Well, Madam Chair, I don't want to rain on the parade of any particular project. Yeah. Okay? They're entitled to go through the RMA process. They're entitled in a market economy to have a go at setting up a business. My line of sight is on what can I do as a politician with a small fund, which Cabinet has to sign off on. I'm just a steward of it.

And this fund can contribute to a broad improvement of energy affordability. So I'm not going to be drawn by the honorable member into talking just about- Yeah, okay... one particular initiative. Yeah. So- I'd rather talk about this fund has endeavored to increase the supply of energy by contributing towards geothermal energy and other projects.

Yeah. So, Scott Willis, you want to bring your question to budget? Yes. The appropriation. So a question on- If you want to talk about energy. Yes. What implications does the reduction in funding for the Regional Just Transition have for the regions, where we've already heard that they're suffering economic hardship, we're having industrial- Sure... crisis, we're having climate impacts happening.
What impact is that- Yeah, okay... reduction? I don't believe in just transition. I think it is climate alarmism and puffery. So I'm totally disinterested in any project called Just Transition.

Yes, supplementary. Yeah. So taken that you believe that climate impacts are not happening, and yet there are extreme weather events, what advice did the minister receive from officials regarding the potential consequences of reducing funding for regional just transition activities?

I think you're being unreasonable. You must have read the briefing. The first $200 million that went from this fund went to Stopbank flood resilience work. You're talking to the politician who found the money that saved Taradale. You're talking to the politician that found the money, prior to being made unemployed in 2020, for the Stopbank work that saved the town of Taradale. And had the money been properly deployed in other parts of Hawke's Bay, in my view, the devastating effects of those adverse weather events.

See, the difference between Mr. Willis and me is I don't deploy all this catastrophic, alarmist language about climate change. I'm the sort of politician that finds the money, gets on, and builds things, and that's the best thing that Kiwi politicians can do, rather than add to a chorus of shrill language that has zero impact on the quality of lives of Kiwis. Our job in infrastructure is to spend the money and boost the resilience of communities by doing practical things.

All right. A new primary from Dr. David Wilson. Minister spent $150 million on water storage. To what benefit do you think? Well, if we all accept that we're vacillating or oscillating between El Niño and La Niña, and we're now going into incredibly dry bout of weather, and vast parts of the Hawke's Bay and parts of the Tairāwhiti are going to be parched.

What is the state to do? What does society expect of the state? Can the market single-handedly Deliver water resilience to vast areas of the productive sector of the eastern seaboard of New Zealand. I say yes. I think it's a tragedy that Bill English's government failed the Hawke's Bay.

The Supreme Court, on the strength of one vote, killed the Ruataniwha project. Bill English could easily have changed the law, the Reserves Act, and breathed life into the Ruataniwha project, and the Hawke's Bay would now have had 100 million cubic liters of water.

But that government chose not to do that. So years later, we're having the same debate, we're hearing the same tired, hackneyed phrases from the left of politics that the world is frying and we've got to shrink our economy. No. There's nothing we can do. We can only invest in resilience and building water storage, so the 150 million, which is a drop in the bucket in my view, is an attempt. And I'll say one thing for Nick Smith, he brought forward a private member's bill. It was not supported by Labor, it was not supported by the Greens. Well, the Greens hate water storage.

It was supported by New Zealand First in 2018. It was a private member's bill. In the absence of that bill, there would be no Waimair water storage. Yes, I know the project was far more expensive than it should've been, but at least they've got water storage. Question from Krisfina Tankiwaimano.

Thank you, Madam Chair. Yeah. Minister, you've mentioned East Coast as a basket case, and after the budget announcement, some people were disappointed that State Highway 35 was not mentioned. Will a portion of the RAF be allocated to supporting resilience on State Highway 35?

Well, my personal view, it's not the government's view, is State Highway 35 should not continue to be managed in the way that it is. I think it should be regarded as a bespoke highway, because it's never ever, in my view, going to pass a certain threshold that we expect for various highways.

I also think that what we should be doing is building a wharf or a jetty or some facility to help the businesses in that area exploit the option of coastal shipping. I accept that it's a controversial option. David Parker almost got there. And this fund, sadly, is not dedicated to roading options. I know that that road certainly needs a lot of care and love, but I just think that over time, and I hope that a future government does treat that particular rohe as requiring a special bespoke approach.

I think that the way in which the forestry rules are being applied up there is excessive. I think also that there's great scope for reintroducing and reigniting more agriculture in certain areas. I'm very encouraged that Poverty Bay can grow again with more water storage, but I sort of feel it needs, from whoever the government might be, a very focused approach to rescue the fortunes of the Tairāwhiti.

And it's going to need more than a flash road. Supplementary, yeah. On that note, you have mentioned forestry. Mm-hmm. Are there any specific wood processing projects from the growth fund that will help support particularly forests north of Tokomaru Bay? Well, we're talking about that rohe from around Cape Runaway to, uh, Tokoroa, Tokomaru Bay.

What in the middle I would've thought, Madam Chair, is needed in New Zealand, because we have to govern the country for the entirety of the multi. Mm-hmm. What we need there, genuinely I believe this, Krisfina, is the reestablishment of something akin to Portawanui. And I think it's a genuine public good expenditure.

I mean, Route 35 is a public good expenditure. Build a port. It will be relatively inexpensive, so if we have produce from that part of New Zealand, it can go out by water. And the difficulty that I see is that unless the Crown remains involved in some of these areas, I just don't think there's a market solution.

I accept my view is not fully shared by all of my coalition partners, but doesn't mean you're not allowed to have a view. But that's not the view at the moment of our cabinet. And that's fine. My job is to implement the cabinet dictates, and at this stage, the fund is not available for the road.

Things might change in the future. And I mean, I have to acknowledge, in Tauranga, they want roads as well. The Bay of Plenty, they're desperate for roads. Up home, we're desperate for roads. I mean, we've got an infrastructure deficit in New Zealand. I don't think we're going to be able to build a hell of a lot more four-lane highways, if you don't mind me saying so, Madam Chair, until we get a better handle on why roads are so expensive.

Mm-hmm. Now, the Greens might say, "Well, thank God you've come to your senses, Shane. We don't need more roads." Well, I'm not quite that radical. All right. Scott, you have a question. Yeah. Coming back to the Ruataniwha dam. Yep. That, is it the government's perspective that it's actually valuable to put money into zombie projects that have already been defeated in the courts, that have already shown that they don't have a lot of community support, and that in fact, instead of Increasing resilience may in fact provide or set people up for greater challenges in

the future, given we've had waterborne outbreaks in that area, given that the increased intensification of dairy is known to create problems in that area. Well, I'll just park up our election debates for another 10 weeks. Yeah. But I would say, I'll go back to Damien O'Connor's question, what really are you on about when you use the term regional development? It is dynamic, and I accept what you're saying that because Ruataniwha

failed, but I don't think it's fair to say that you should still judge a project as being zombied because the Supreme Court voted against it in 2000, whenever Bill English was around. What year was that? 2015 or '16. Circumstances have evolved, so each government has to make a call based on the vicissitudes of the time that it's governing in.

And I do believe the Tukituki project, which I think is the old Ruataniwha project, is still a valid economic proposition. Question from Kushla Tangaidi. E Te Minita, you probably more than anyone in this room understand the complexities of dealing with Mana Whenua and iwi in scenarios like this, and I have had concerns from whanau around Ruataniwha about this project. How will your government manage those relationships?

Yeah. That's a valid point. So yesterday, I had a meeting with Tamatea, the Taiwhenua. And I do feel that at an operational skirmish level, there are a lot of sore points in terms of water projects with the Tangata Whenua, but my plea to them is lift it up to the strategic level.

Surely, the strategic war that the Hawke's Bay is involved in, if you do not have water when you are facing the statistical probability that the temperatures in your area are going to lead to more droughts, lead to more parched landscapes, surely to maintain the productivity of your pastoral-based, land-based economy requires you to have access to water. If the groundwater is finite, but Mother Nature does do these drops of water from time to

time, then capture it. So that's the call, and if the community is divided, then what does the Crown do? Does it intervene? Because over time, the cost will fall on the taxpayers. So I say as a part of the government I belong to, it's a defensible decision for the Crown to make to intervene and lead the outcome of water storage.

And for the people who think that that's heretical and we have no mandate, meet me at the ballot box. All right. We have Ramrey from Dr. David Woodside. Minister, you started off by mentioning the difference between grants, debt, and co-funding, difficulties in those arrangements.

What do you think we've learnt from the Regional Infrastructure Fund as a whole in that regard? Well, look, let's all be honest with each other. The last government, if I can use, and in a very professional way, if I can use as an example the former Minister of Energy.

She championed an intervention, where money was redirected from the ETS auctions, and that money was deployed to meet the costs of the zero transition. And amongst other areas where the money was directed was the steel mill, I think some of the dairy investments in the South Island.

And then a new government came in that I'm a part of, and the sense was that was not an appropriate role for the Crown to intervene with Crown money. Then we set up a fund where our party, as a small part of the current government, did believe that Crown intervention from time to time is appropriate.

And, I won't walk away from the fact that there have been times of tension because you have to pass a high threshold as to whether or not you can justify the intervention. And that is why the intervention is based around predominantly co-investment.

But to do co-investment, the other side need to go and mobilize the capital, and to do that, the staff have to take a damn very protracted and calculated approach to make sure that the deal is real. I'm not saying that you be reckless with subsidy and grant money, but at the end of the day, there's a certain threshold, the door goes out the door.

But you are waiting when other people are mobilizing capital, it may be international capital that they're raising. So I think the lesson that we've learned is that if you have the co-investment approach- You are inevitably going to take a lot longer, because you're not in control of the process purely yourself. Other people are in control of the process on their side of the ledger.

Mm-hmm. That's probably one of the learnings. Question, Kushla Daghani Manuel. Thank you. Tena koe, Minister. Maori economic development is a priority area of this fund. $40 million was mentioned. Do you think that's adequate, and how are the outcomes being measured?

Well, I think the figure is probably a bit higher than what I've articulated. But if I can just refer to some of the projects. What we've tried to do is focus in some areas in rural New Zealand on energy. So, some of the projects, for example, in the Bay of Plenty, they're in pretty isolated areas.

And often, it's been the lines companies, the power lines have said, "Look, can you work with us in the local Maori communities and do solar projects?" So that when the bad weather strikes, and perhaps this is what Mr. Willis was talking about. Maybe that's a small example of just transition.

Embed a solar project, I guess, with batteries. Mm. So, if you have crappy weather, the system fails, there's a local source of energy. And because in some of these rural areas, the only damn thing that's in existence is a marae. There are no community halls. There is no rugby club. So, that's an example.

And of course, there have been other projects where we've defined our infrastructure as being marae infrastructure, because in some of these areas, that's all there is. That's excited some of the scribblers on Instagram who feel that that's racist expenditure. But once again, meet me at the ballot box. There's got to be some balance in the allocation of these funds and how we define infrastructure.

It looks like, Minister, those are the questions that members had for today. I think there might be some follow-up questions later on. So now we move to the next portfolio of yours, the resources. We allow a couple of minutes if there's going to be any change of any official. Right. Thank you.
Okay. I
forget we had today.
Six months.
We are starting with resources- Yeah... and then we will go on. Thank you. Okay. Well, given I've got off to an awkward start about the confusion over the
timing,
yeah, just presume that you've had a look at the material. Now, got the officials with me if the questions get too finicky.

Amongst other things, as I said, and I'll repeat it again, the role covers off not only the oil and gas sector, my attempt to lead a reinvigoration of the mineral sector. A small role in terms of fuel security, and a role in the sense that my views are sought from time to time

in the fast track process if, for example, a project emerges in the fast track where MBIE may be contacted to give a view about a minerals project. But look, other than that, I'll just take questions. Thank you, Minister. We'll start with Honorable Megan Woods.

Thank you, Minister. Just the first line of questioning, just would like an update on the critical minerals framework with the US- Yeah... and what the stage is with that, what the timeline's looking like there. Right. Well, last year-- Madam Chair, if you can give me three to five minutes, because I just want to- Yes, that's fine... skip us through. Now, and I'm certainly not going to shy away from answering this question.

No, that's fine. Be useful. So last year, and I'll need some help from the officials. Last year, New Zealand committed to a supply framework Which has since been called FORGE. Now, what the hell was that formerly called? Mineral Security Partnership. Right, Mineral Security Partnership, formerly a collectivity chaired by Korea, and we committed to it, Minister, Honorable Member Woods.

The chairmanship, I think, is shifting to America. That is well underway. Late last year, MB and Foreign Affairs begun to engage with America. There have been moderate levels of contact between Japan and Korea. On the latter, I want the level of intensity to grow as soon as the Korean ambassador is appointed to New Zealand.

I'm not sure when that's going to happen. Obviously, I feel the intensity of contact will grow once the American ambassador arrives here. That's going to happen very soon. I have not taken a Cabinet paper forward to sign off any bilateral deal with America.

It is my intention to do so, but Foreign Affairs and MB haven't given me such a paper. I don't mind telling, Madam Chair, my fellow parliamentarians, the real sticky bit is should New Zealand commit to a floor price? By that, Madam Chair, if we are to try and boost our capacity to have critical minerals as an industry in New Zealand, whilst knowing that it is largely dominated by a longstanding set of very powerful interests in the mineral

sector coming out of China, who have the ability to change, through powerful influences, the clearing price. And projects and businesses, Madam Chair, do have to shutter or actually close down because they can't match the clearing price when the price drops.

One option that the Americans have promoted in Australia with their deal is a guaranteed floor price. Now, I don't have the support of Cabinet for a guaranteed floor price that someone in New Zealand would have to underwrite. So that remains-- That's why I asked you for three to five minutes.

That remains an outstanding issue because I'm advised, and I'm pretty sure Susan behind me will tell me that I'm saying the right thing, that potentially offends or violates various trade agreements that we've committed to. So, I don't have anything more to add until that issue is resolved.

I don't see myself being in a position to take any prospective deal to my colleagues. That's probably the most lucid thing I can say to you as my colleagues, parliamentary speaking. Thank you very much for that. Just to clarify, so the not having the Cabinet support for a floor price that's underwritten, that would be a government underwrite, would it be?

Well, the way I see it as the promoter, well, obviously Winston Peters is involved as well, but I've taken on the role of doing the heavy lifting. If I look at the Australian option, the Australian option is that they do have something akin to a floor price. Well, who's going to pay for that?

Sure. And it's a government underwrite. Well, at this stage, it is not government policy in New Zealand- Yeah... to have a government underwrite. Yeah. I have to stress that is not policy of the New Zealand Government. It may be the policy of the Australian government, but it's not the policy of the New Zealand government. So have you asked officials for advice on a government underwrite in the New Zealand context, understanding it's not current policy?

Yeah. So this progress, sorry, this process has been progressing for, what the hell is this month? For about four or five months, and there's been a lot of toing and froing. You might imagine that the people responsible for consistency with trade obligations, they've been studying these things and toing and froing for months on end.

Thank you. So just in terms of the timeline, I think it was in early February that a draft framework went to Cabinet around it. Then on the 18th of February, there was the $18 million ring fence from the Regional Infrastructure Fund around supporting critical mineral projects. Was this linked to the work that was going on in terms of the critical minerals framework, and was that ring-fencing of funding discussed with the US?

So, what was the American guy's name? Michael... Koon. Susan? Koon. Dosomba. Hmm? Dosomba. Michael Dosomba. Yeah, Dosomba. So, the undersecretary, we can give you the proper name. He came down here about six weeks ago. We had a meeting here in Parliament with a bunch of stakeholders. He brought a high-level delegation from America.

Within that interaction between myself, a bunch of mineral companies, reps from the Japanese Embassy, the Korean Embassy, the Aussie High Commission I gave a briefing about the $80 million RIF fund. Mm-hmm. But in terms of a dedicated negotiation over that fund with the Americans, no, that was a Cabinet decision, quite separate from negotiations pertaining to a potential deal with the Americans. But when Michael out of Washington came here, I gave a briefing-

Sure... in front of all and sundry to him and his delegation. So in those discussions with the US late last year and early this year, prior to the February Cabinet paper, was the need for New Zealand to have some government backing of critical minerals discussed?

I don't know. I'd need to check. I regarded the RIF fund. I regarded the RIF fund. So how it arose. Yeah, I don't think it's fair to say that it was driven by the Americans. It was largely driven by myself. I found myself in a situation where I took a proposal to my colleagues, if we wanted, in New Zealand, largely in the West Coast, but not exclusively.

If we wanted in New Zealand, a critical minerals industry that was struggling to monetize its operations with outside capital, then I believed, as we did with the goldmine in Reefton, our government, I believed that we should de-risk it. Now, it took a while to develop that, I must confess, because it meant Crown intervention, and there were elements amongst my colleagues who doubted that was an appropriate role, and fair enough.

But we did have a precedent in Jacinda's government by putting $20 million into a goldmine. So, that took a while to develop, and I'd been talking about it for quite a while as the Minerals Minister. So, I don't think it's fair to say it was driven by whatever discussions had been happening in Washington.

So have you- It was la- it was largely driven by myself. Have any projects been funded out of that ring-fenced $80 million yet? No. What we've done, we haven't funded any projects. We opened up an application process, and we have received applications, and we are now at a point of finalizing with successful applicants, um, terms and conditions, but we're not at the stage at a point where we can publicly declare them until such time we've agreed terms and conditions.

But, um, uh, Madam Chair, it remains my view it's a legitimate and defensible way of committing Crown resources to accelerate these types of, um, of enterprises. A supplementary from Dr. David Wilson. Uh, thank you, Minister. Um, what are the national and/or regional benefits from exploration to critical minerals?

Well, it depends how far, um, you spread them. There is an entity called Aspiring. They're um... I don't know if you'd define them as critical minerals, but they've been working with our officials. Um, they're fairly well known. Then there are the entities down there in the Pipituna. Tungsten, monazite, garnet.

So they will create jobs, but the level of processing you can do in New Zealand is actually limited because of the negative externalities associated with intense processing in New Zealand. I just don't feel that Kiwis will put up with the environmental footprint of the full range of processing that's required.

Some semi-processing perhaps. I just have my doubts that Kiwis would, um, be able to tolerate the full range of negative externalities that the Chinese, for example, are willing to wear. Right. A new question, Steve Edward. Thank you. Uh, Madam Chair, in terms of your ambition to grow the value of mineral exports- Mm-hmm... and to the point you've just made about the negative externalities.

Yeah. Forest and Bird have just published a map of the, uh, conservation estate that's, um, impacted by the, the new proposed opening of 60% of it to potential exploitation and sale. That's largely driven by that ambition- I can't see that... for minerals.

I, I can't see what issue- Sorry. Or, or just, yes. It's just a map of the areas, um, that the government's proposing to open up- That's right... in conservation estate. And the, the major driver of that is your desire for an increase in critical minerals extraction. Mm. Is it your understanding that the large cache of those potential minerals exist in conservation estate?

Is that why there is a push by the government to do this opening up of those particular areas? Okay. I, I, look, I'm aware of a, a newspaper article. Donald dodged the question. Um, and Tama said he's gonna go back and possibly reverse the decision, but now I know the value of the decision, I might oppose Tama doing that.

I had no idea that was the effect. I don't think it's fair, Madam Chair, to say you can't blame my advocacy for the minerals sector. I just happen to believe that the DOC estate is too big I happen to believe since 1987 that Kiwis are on my side, that we need to create more opportunities for economic development within 38% of New Zealand's landscape.

And I do accept that it's heresy for some members of Parliament, but I just look at the state of our economy, and I think it's time that we exploit part of that 38%. I might be right, I might be wrong. We'll know at the ballot box. Right. We go to, Honorable Megan Woods.

Thank you. Just going back to the critical- Yes... minerals framework. So, from what I've gleaned from public sources, negotiations restarted in April with the US. Right. Where are we up to? Re-engagement. Yep. Yeah. Re-engagement negotiations restarted in April.

Is the July deadline for the US still running? Where are we up to there? I mean, negotiations are still underway is the short version. I don't think there is a- That's right... would that be right, Kate? That's right. There's no particular deadline we've been set. So have the US softened on the July deadline in terms of what- They seem to be a bit distracted... in terms of the tariff penalties?

Well, I'll give you a straight-up answer. We can get an answer sooner rather than later. I'd like to tidy it up before the election, but we can only run as fast as they can operate. Sure. Well, yeah, we can put that in writing. That's fine. So after, the re-engagement or negotiations restarting in April, accepting that the RIF had been something you'd been, the ring-fence and the RIF had been something you'd been thinking about- Mm... prior to the February talks.

Yeah. And since this April re-engagement or any other engagement, have any possible projects within the $80 million ring-fence been discussed? You know, I think we had a really good meeting, eh? What was the date when Michael came to have a meeting in Parliament? Was it, April or May? It was mid-April. Yeah.

Okay, so mid-April, the American delegation came down. I mean, this guy's very senior. We can give you the details. So we had, I don't know, half a dozen firms who are already mining. They're out there raising capital. He gave an indication of how you gain access to the Congress-mandated capital. We told them to deal with the American Embassy.

He's since emailed back with various people who may be interested in New Zealand. I don't think much has come of it. So it's all been fairly transparent. These entities that are busy in New Zealand, some of them have listed on the Kiwi share market. Are they likely to gain access to any of that American capital?

I hope they do, but it's going to be hard yakka, because the types of projects they're looking at genuinely are of a larger scale. Sure. A supplementary to that. So was co-funding from the $80 million ring-fenced amount discussed- Yeah, so-... for those projects?

Yeah. So the deal is that in the event that those current mineral operators can source private capital, which they're meant to be doing anyway, and crowd in money from other sources, then the RIF fund is there to help de-risk capital mobilization, and it's premised on the notion that the New Zealand Government is an eager participant to see the footprint grow,

but not to totally guarantee, as we have guaranteed, for example, bank loans in our recent budget initiative. It's to assist in crowding capital into mining initiatives. Thank you. So just to confirm, there is a live set of possibilities that the- Yes, every day I'm encouraging that.

Yeah. Every day. Yeah. That from that $80 million allocation, there could be projects that include conditions linked to the US framework and/or the US public finance that is on offer with that. It just depends, Megan, on what deals they can pull together, and as I said, we haven't announced anything, because we haven't seen any evidence to date that there's any live deals out there.

So will there be, I mean, as part of that discussion, offtake commitments, preferred access, links to supply chains in the US? Have those discussions been had? Yeah. Well, therein lies the problem. We haven't got a deal to ink. I haven't got a paper to take- Yeah... to Cabinet, because the Americans haven't responded to our officials with any level of exactitude that I'm in possession of information that I can take to our cabinet.

So will you release details of that before any deal would be signed? Well, you've asked two things there. I'd take some advice from the foreign affairs as to what can and can't be released before I go to, Cabinet. And in relation to commercial deals, well, I'd have to take advice from the architects of those deals as to what's appropriate for release. I wouldn't want to either imperil the Crown or indeed compromise my fellow New Zealanders in terms of what they're trying to put together.

All right. New primary, Steven. Thank you, Madam Chair. Minister, in terms of the specific critical minerals that-... are of interest to international markets- Aye is it your understanding that the United States has such a high interest in vanadium and antimony because they are essential in the manufacture of military hardware?

Well, I don't want to dodge the question, but I'm advised that antimony, which is something that raw gold is very interested in. Mm. And vanadium, sadly, is a project that's marooned because of these muppets up in Taranaki. Hopefully the applicants have another go at it.

But I don't think that you can stigmatize those two genuine mineral properties just because the final use may or may not end up being a part of the military complex. And I think this is what distinguishes the Green Party from my party in terms of politics.

Do you think that New Zealanders would be comfortable with-... conservation estate being given over to mining to produce minerals that could end up as part of the American war machine? Well, that's a very emotive and classic sort of Vietnam War, outmoded expression of language.

That's the practical reality of what we're looking at, though, isn't it? All right. No, no, no. But I think Kiwis want jobs. I think Kiwis want development. I think Kiwis want to know whoever the business is, Mr. Abel, operating out of our country, they're obeying the law. Now, if they sell to other businesses operating in the US, EU, wherever, providing no one, like the business, the rocket business out of Mahia Peninsula, as long as they haven't broken the law, I don't really care.

You don't- All right, all right. Questions to the Chair. Supplementary, Dr. Vanessa Baden. Thank you, Madam Chair. Thank you, Minister Jones. I understand antimony's also used in hardening plates and lead acid batteries, and also in semiconductors, which are used in electronics.

Do you think that New Zealanders want to see more energy storage through batteries? And should we be participating in processes that lead to more battery storage and more electronic uses? Yeah. These rare hitherto unknown minerals in New Zealand have a myriad of values, whether it's monazite, tungsten, garnet.

So I just shy away from catastrophizing the limited military implications. Mm. There's energy, there's medical purposes, there's industrial purposes. But, I don't deny, doctor, that there may from time to time be military purposes. Mm. But technology has multiple outputs.

Mm. And what previously wasn't military, today might be military. Mm. Supplementary. Supplementary, Stephen. Yeah. So, 80% of the vanadium supply is controlled by China and Russia, which is why the US is so anxious to get its own supply. Would you, in the context of the previous question, be happy to say that we are only prepared to supply the likes of these minerals for specific clean energy uses, not for military uses, if there's such a narrow part of what they're going to be used for?

Well, no, I'm not in a position to dictate who and how investors end up operating their businesses. They must obey the law. Now, if there are various export bans, if there are various guardrails, then they have to obey them. But look, I just want to sort of bring a dose of reality here.

If we do not want the footprint in New Zealand of extracting these critical minerals, but we still need them, and we're going to draw them from countries that have a worse environmental record- Mm... how is that better for the globe? So why is it and what is it that gives us the sense of moral green superiority that we'll keep our virginity here, but we'll quietly buy things from other areas where they have a more s****y reputation than we do?

I don't do my politics like that. That's what separates you and I. Mm. All right. Any more questions? I've got a follow-up. Yeah, yeah. I wonder in regard to acquiring resources- Yep... what opportunities or advice you have sought on resource recovery opportunities for getting the likes of these minerals.

For example- There's 800 times more gold in a ton of electronic waste than there is in a ton of Coromandel hard rock. And that's a highly unexplored opportunity for e-waste recovery where minerals have already been extracted, and before they go into landfill, they could be reacquired and utilized and be of great value economically.

Okay. Well, no, I haven't looked into that. But I guess I'd say to you all, the market's not prohibiting that from happening, so why is the market not delivering that outcome? New question from Honorable Megan Woods. Thank you. I am going to change tack, but members might want to come back to that.

I just want to talk about the $200 million gas fund- Yeah... that was set up. How many proposals are actively being considered at the moment? Well, look, last year, the Supreme Court, as you may know, I'm not happy with the Supreme Court. They made a decision, Madam Chair, I think it's called the Aotearoa Youth Climate or something.

It was a decision that was passed in November or December, which requires the minister to have quite an extensive appraisal of climate change implications when allocating or considering petroleum permit applications. And then, broadly speaking, Madam Chair, it requires a far more expansive consideration of tangata whenua interests beyond the local Taranaki hapū.

Now, I don't like that decision, and sadly, I've run out of time for us to pass legislation to outlaw that decision. I see that as judicial adventurism. And I'm having to wade through that decision as to whether or not the implications of that decision represents some kind of obstacle to rapidly allocating the pūtea from the $200 million.

We haven't signed any applications off. We have a huge number of people who are interested in accessing it. It does include people keen on investing and storing gas. The Gen Taylors, not surprisingly, seem to be disinterested in meeting the capital costs of storing gas.

So I'm keen to sign those off well before the election. But I don't mind revealing here, Madam Chair, which hasn't been stated publicly yet, I don't mind revealing here that we've had to work out what are the implications of that Supreme Court decision on decisions we may take, and will be taking, over this $200 million fund.

Are there any applications that are sitting there awaiting decision, once you've waded through the court decision, that are from overseas companies? I'm not sure. I think there's one. I haven't actually had a full list because I've kept aloof from it for fear of me knowing who these people are.

What we've got is we've got a wee committee of three experts, and then we've got MBIE, and then there's a couple of ministers, I think is how it works. Then they're eventually going to come to us. So I haven't had, to the best of my knowledge, I might be proven wrong, but Catherine, I don't think I've seen a full list to date of the applicants. I'm not entirely sure how many of them have an international connection. Some of them might.

But where people have been to see me and they're keen to express an interest to get access to the money, I send them straight to Susan and the people all around me here. Sure. Do officials want to comment on that? Yeah, I think there's one applicant that's keen that has connections with the UK. But other than that, I don't know.

Thank you. You said there's a committee of three experts. Who are- Yeah, there's Andy Knight, not Parisini, who's the other fella? Jordan Bagani. Jordan Bagani, and who's the other fella? Tim Allen. Tim Allen. Right. Okay. In terms of biogas and any projects that are around biogas, are they being considered within that fund?

Linked in biogas? There are no biogas applications that we have at the moment. Cool. Now, I asked you in a written question, Minister, around whether or not you'd sought or received Crown Law or Treasury advice around commercial liability or indemnity risks linked to the- On what risk?

For commercial liability or indemnity risks that were linked to any projects. You said that your officials had engaged with Treasury and Crown Law in developing advice around the fund. Are you or your officials able to give us any update around what that liability or indemnity risk for the Crown might look like?

Thank you. So we generally use external legal advice for commercial risks to deal with commercial risks. Each project we assess, we do a risk assessment on the full range of risks, so not just commercial liability. When we present those decisions to ministers, we cover those.

Thank you. How the system has worked, but I don't mind saying that MB have taken their time on this issue. There's obviously a lot of judiciousness at play. They've certainly been a lot more judicious than I would've been, because partly it's a significant amount of money, and they are working amongst other people.

Obviously, Andy Knight is fairly well known to members on this committee. He is an expert. And we know that it's contested space. It's highly contested space. But it's my view that there can be more gas squeezed figuratively out of the geological sponge.

But it has worked out to be a tad slower than I would've liked, but I'm trusting in a machine that we have delegated the mahu to. It's not driven just by one politician. But I do hope to take decisions forward to colleagues well before the election.

Got a sub one there. Thank you. All right. Just- Yeah. I've just got one more sub. Yeah. So, in terms of looking at alternatives for that, and, Minister, you said that Kiwis want jobs. Couldn't agree more. Mm. We've discussed deals with the US, we've discussed a $200 million fund that we're dealing with overseas companies and how they may get a handout from the government around that.

Have you asked for advice around how we might be able to develop energy security ourselves here and create Kiwi jobs around things like the creation of, for example, biomass and torrefied pellets- Yeah... in the central North Island, and are you willing to pivot that fund to start actually backing Kiwi companies and Kiwi workers around that? And will we see decisions coming out of that $200 million fund backing them rather than overseas companies?

So, I will gladly get the officials to write you a letter, because that was the presentation of the last scrutiny session. The money that's come out of the RIFT fund dedicated to those very projects. No, I'm talking about the $200 million fund. Yeah. The RIFT fund- Yeah... has already spent a considerable amount of money on energy projects of the nature that you just described.

Okay. But that $200 million fund- This $200 million fund is for gas And overseas companies that are currently talking to you about it. Well, no overseas company has been given any money from this fund, and you've got to stop catastrophizing- No, but you're in talks with the minister... way ahead of doing that. But you're in talks with the minister. Well, I've talked with everyone. I'm the prince of the provinces.

I've talked with all and sundry. All right. Well, the prince of the global multinationals. Okay. A question from Steve Abel. Thank you, Madam Chair. Just to get confirmation on that fund. This is a quick sub on that, but I've got a bigger sub. Biogas is eligible for that fund? Biogas are eligible for... What's the answer?

The limitation is natural gas. Yeah. Natural gas. So that includes biogas? Potentially. Like I say, we haven't had any applications, so we haven't done that assessment. Okay. Cool. So my primary question is, Minister, you passed legislation. We put to the first... Or we passed it, actually, legislation on transparency around the gas reserves.

There is a tradition around the world of the oil and gas industry overstating its proven reserves, for obvious reasons, because the value of the industry is tied up in those. Do you have any advice on whether there has been an overstatement of what the gas reserves are, an overestimation or an overbooking of proven reserves?

Well, I think what the advice shows is that the decline is more precipitous than originally anticipated. But I'd be loathed to say it was a deliberate, conspiratorial attempt to overstate it. The facts show that the decline has been steeper than what was originally anticipated.

I wonder how do you assure yourself that there hasn't been an intentional overstating, given that there is the likes of Anadarko, Shell Oil, there's numerous companies around the world, have been fined for overstating their proven reserves. How do you assure yourself that that's not what's been going on here in New Zealand, given the significant consequences for industry of an expectation that there was more gas than there actually is?

Yeah. Well, let's just agree to disagree. I mean, all I can do is go off the facts that MB put in front of me. And, there's no shortage of people who are of the view that there is more gas, geologically speaking, in the broad seascape and landscape of New Zealand. And I think a key difference between me and people perhaps on our side of the government, and the philosophy that you and Megan reflect, is that you have an impoverished view about the gas potential, and we have a more confident view

We just agree to disagree. All right. A new question from Honorable Megan Woods. Thank you. Minister, you talked earlier in the term around doing work around raising royalties- Yep... on Crown minerals. Have you advanced that work stream? Yes. The officials have done quite a bit of work on options. Well, what would options look like to change the royalty regime, and sharing the flow of those royalties with the regions. However, I've chosen not to advance that work until after the election. I don't mind sharing with you, Madam Chair, it was my

sense that we're better off, as a government, this was my advice to my key colleagues, to lessen the uncertainty associated with changing that regime. Rather, I would take the role of boosting the desirability of crowding more investment into the mineral sector, and then dealing after the election with how much and to what extent that regime should change, and have the pūtea redirected to the regions.

So, in getting advice and thinking through the policy changes that would be needed there, did you get feedback from either mining or oil and gas companies that they would withdraw their investments if royalties were raised? I don't think anyone really said that they were going to b****r off, but whenever, Madam Chair, there's a hint that the Crown is going to change tax rates, if I can use that term loosely, you're always going to get

frowns and dark responses. But the Crown is entitled to change levy structures if they feel there's a compelling need. But that won't be happening, if indeed it ever does happen, until after the election. So it's a- Final supplementary from Dr. David Lewis. Final supplementary from Dr.

David Lewis. Thank you, Chair. Yes, Minister. We've spent an awful lot of time on minerals. We're getting into things like biogas and biomass and torrefied pellets, largely unproven at an industrial level. But are there other resources that you think New Zealand has that we could utilize to our benefit for energy? Look, the final thing I'd say is that, and obviously we need to invite officials, we need to invite opposition politicians. I'm hosting a very large hydrogen event in Christchurch. I think one of the lessons from the

Palmerston Street debacle is there is a case for boosting the capacity of hydrogen to add resilience to the fuel situation in New Zealand. And one thing that's bedeviled me personally is the inability to create a legislative response to simplify the creation and the growth of the hydrogen sector.

I wanted to nationalize the hydrogen resource. Naturally, that ran into treaty claims, so that didn't go anywhere. And if there's some way of simplifying the ability of investors to gather permits to exclusively harness hydrogen, simply, I think that's a new frontier.

There is white hydrogen. I'm not a physicist. I don't have a background in chemistry, but obviously I have a business background. So, that was a simple example where I think there is a grand case for that. All right. We'll conclude this hearing there. Thank you so much, Minister. Thank you, Minister.
And there might be some follow-up questions, written questions. Thank you.
